title: How to Use Intuition When Making Financial Decisions
meta_description: Discover how to trust your gut with money. Learn practical steps to blend intuition with financial decisions without ignoring logic.
og_title: How to Use Intuition When Making Financial Decisions
og_description: Discover how to trust your gut with money. Learn practical steps to blend intuition with financial decisions without ignoring logic.
body:
You've Been Told Your Gut is Wrong. It's Not.

Let me guess. You've made a financial decision based on a feeling before. Maybe you passed on a job that looked perfect on paper because something felt off. Or you bought a stock everyone told you was a bad idea, and it worked out. Or maybe the opposite happened - you ignored that tight feeling in your chest, took the "sensible" path, and it blew up in your face.

If that's you, you're not broken. You're not irresponsible. You're just wired differently. And the world has been telling you your whole life that your wiring is a defect. It's not. It's a feature. The problem isn't your intuition. It's that nobody taught you how to use it in practical, real-world situations - especially when money is involved.

Money is one of the most charged areas for sensitive people. It's wrapped up in survival, self-worth, control, and fear. Your nervous system knows this. So when you sit down to make a financial decision, your intuition doesn't go quiet - it goes loud. The question is whether you can tell the difference between intuition and fear. Right?!

That's what we're going to talk about today. Not woo-woo magic. Not "just trust the universe." Real, grounded, practical ways to use your intuitive sensitivity when making financial decisions - without throwing logic out the window.

Intuition isn't a replacement for thinking. It's a compass that tells you which direction to point your thinking. The problem isn't that you feel too much. It's that you've been taught to ignore what you feel.

Why Your Intuition Gets Louder Around Money

Here's the thing about intuition: it's not some mystical superpower that only appears during full moons. It's a survival mechanism. Your nervous system is constantly scanning your environment for threats and opportunities. It processes information faster than your conscious mind can keep up. That "gut feeling" is just your system sending you a summary report before your brain has finished reading the fine print.

Money triggers this hard because money is tied to safety. When you're making a financial decision - whether it's investing, taking a new job, buying a house, or even just deciding how much to spend on a vacation - your nervous system treats it like a life-or-death situation. That's why you get that knot in your stomach. That's why you can't sleep. That's why you second-guess everything.

But here's the catch: your nervous system doesn't always know the difference between a real threat and a perceived one. Past trauma, family conditioning, and societal messages about money can all distort your intuitive signal. So the first step isn't to blindly follow every feeling. It's to learn how to clean up the signal.

The Two Types of Intuitive Financial Signals

In my experience, there are two main ways intuition shows up around money. You'll probably recognize one or both.

Type 1: The "Hell No" Signal

This one is unmistakable. It's a tightness in your chest, a sinking feeling in your stomach, a sudden urge to walk away. It's the feeling you get when something is wrong even though you can't explain why. This signal is almost always accurate - but only if you haven't already talked yourself out of listening to it. The problem is that most of us have been trained to override this signal with logic. "But the numbers look good." "But everyone says this is a smart move." "But I'll look stupid if I back out now." That's the override. And it's usually a mistake.

Type 2: The "Quiet Yes" Signal

This one is harder to catch. It's not a big dramatic feeling. It's a subtle sense of expansion, a lightness, a feeling of "this is fine" that doesn't need to be justified. It's the opposite of the frantic energy that comes with a bad decision. When you get a quiet yes around a financial choice, you don't feel the need to convince yourself or anyone else. You just know. This signal is easy to miss because it doesn't scream at you. It whispers. And if you're used to living in a state of anxiety, you might not even notice it's there.

So how do you start telling these two apart? You practice. You start small. You don't jump straight into a six-figure investment decision and try to use your gut. You start with everyday choices.

Start Small: Train Your Intuition Like a Muscle

If you want to use intuition for big financial decisions, you have to build trust with it on small ones first. This is non-negotiable. You wouldn't run a marathon without training, right? Same deal here.

Here's a simple practice I recommend to every sensitive person I work with:

For one week, make every small financial decision based on how it feels. Not what it costs. Not what you "should" do. Just how it feels.

  • Buying coffee? Check in with your body first. Does this purchase feel expansive or constrictive?
  • Choosing between two brands at the grocery store? Pause for three seconds and see which one your hand naturally reaches for.
  • Deciding whether to order takeout or cook? Notice the energy behind each option. One probably feels heavy. One feels lighter.

Do this for a week. Don't judge the results. Just notice. What you're doing is recalibrating your nervous system's relationship with money. You're teaching yourself that it's safe to feel your feelings around financial choices. You're building evidence that your intuition is reliable.

After a week, you'll start to notice patterns. Maybe you realize that when you ignore the "hell no" signal, you always regret it. Maybe you notice that the "quiet yes" always leads to a better outcome than you expected. That's the data you need to start trusting yourself.

The Logic-Feeling Balance: You Don't Have to Choose

One of the biggest myths about intuition is that it's the opposite of logic. It's not. They're partners. Logic gives you the map. Intuition tells you which path to take.

When you're making a financial decision, do the logical work first. Crunch the numbers. Research the options. Talk to the experts. Get all the data. But don't stop there. After you've done the logical work, sit with the decision. Close your eyes. Take three deep breaths. And ask yourself: How does this actually feel in my body?

If the logic says "yes" but your body says "no," don't ignore it. Something is off. Maybe you don't have all the information yet. Maybe there's a risk you haven't considered. Maybe your timing is wrong. Don't force it. Give yourself permission to wait.

If the logic says "no" but your body says "yes," that's trickier. This is where most people get stuck. But here's the thing: sometimes your intuition sees opportunities that your logical mind can't yet justify. That doesn't mean you should throw caution to the wind. It means you should investigate further. Ask more questions. Look for evidence that supports the feeling. And if you can't find any, trust the feeling anyway - but start small. Don't bet the farm on a hunch. Dip your toe in first.

There's a book that helped me understand this balance better than anything else. It's called Thinking, Fast and Slow by Daniel Kahneman. It's not a spiritual book - it's about how the brain makes decisions. But it explains exactly why your intuition works the way it does. If you're serious about this stuff, it's worth your time. See on Amazon (paid link)

How to Clean Up Your Intuitive Signal

Your intuition is like a radio station. But if your nervous system is full of static - from past money trauma, family patterns, or societal pressure - you're going to have a hard time hearing the signal clearly. Here are three ways to clean up the static.

1. Get Grounded Before You Decide

Never make a financial decision when you're stressed, anxious, or emotionally triggered. Your nervous system will hijack the process and you'll make choices based on fear, not clarity. Before you make any decision, do something to ground yourself. Take five slow breaths. Put your feet on the floor. Go for a walk. Splash cold water on your face. Wait until your heart rate settles and your mind feels clear. Then decide.

2. Separate Your Feelings from Your Family's Feelings

A lot of what you think is your intuition is actually your parents' voice. If you grew up with scarcity mentality - "money is hard to come by," "rich people are greedy," "you have to work yourself to death to earn a living" - those messages are probably playing on a loop in your subconscious. When you feel a tightness around a financial decision, ask yourself: Is this my feeling, or is this my mother's feeling? You'd be surprised how often it's not yours.

3. Practice Non-Attachment

Intuition works best when you're not desperate. When you desperately need a decision to turn out a certain way, your nervous system gets cloudy. You start seeing what you want to see, not what's actually there. The antidote is non-attachment. Tell yourself: I don't have to get this right. I can handle whatever happens. This is just one decision among many. That loosens the grip of fear and lets your intuition speak more clearly.

Real-Life Examples: What This Looks Like in Practice

Let me give you a couple of examples so you can see how this works in real life.

Example 1: The Job Offer That Felt Wrong

Sarah came to me because she was offered a job that paid 30% more than her current role. The company had great reviews. The benefits were amazing. Everyone in her life told her to take it. But every time she thought about signing the offer letter, her stomach would clench. She felt nauseous. She couldn't sleep.

Her logical mind said yes. Her body said no. She took the job anyway because she "didn't want to be irrational." Three months in, she realized why her body was screaming at her. The company culture was toxic. Her boss was a micromanager. The high salary came with burnout-level expectations. She quit six months later, having lost more in time and health than she gained in money.

If she had paused and asked her body what it was trying to tell her, she might have saved herself a lot of pain. The "hell no" signal was accurate. She just didn't trust it.

Example 2: The Investment That Felt Quietly Right

Marcus had been saving for years to start a small business. He found a mentor who offered to partner with him. The numbers were tight - it was a risk. But when Marcus sat with the decision, he didn't feel the frantic "hell no" energy. He felt a quiet sense of peace. Not excitement, not certainty, just... okayness. Like the decision was already made and he was just catching up to it.

He took the leap. It wasn't easy. There were hard months. But the business grew slowly and steadily. Looking back, Marcus says the quiet yes was the most reliable signal he'd ever gotten. It wasn't flashy. It was just true.

If you want to go deeper into how to recognize these signals, I highly recommend The Gift of Fear by Gavin de Becker. It's technically about personal safety, but it's one of the best books ever written on how intuition works in practical, high-stakes situations. See on Amazon (paid link)

When Not to Trust Your Intuition

I'm not going to tell you that your intuition is always right. It's not. Sometimes your gut feeling is actually just fear dressed up as wisdom. Sometimes it's old trauma. Sometimes it's a bad burrito. You need to know when to trust it and when to question it.

Here are the red flags that your "intuition" might actually be something else:

  • It feels frantic or urgent. Real intuition is calm. It doesn't scream. If you feel a panicked need to act immediately, that's fear, not intuition. Slow down.
  • It's based on scarcity. If your gut is saying "this is your only chance" or "you'll never get another opportunity like this," that's usually a trap. Real opportunities don't require you to abandon your discernment.
  • It contradicts your values. If a decision feels "right" but it would require you to compromise your integrity, that's not intuition. That's wishful thinking.
  • You can't explain it to yourself. Intuition doesn't always come with a reason, but it should come with a feeling of clarity. If you feel confused, that's not intuition. That's noise.

When in doubt, wait. Give yourself 24 hours. Sleep on it. Revisit the decision when you're calm and grounded. If the feeling is still there, it's worth listening to. If it fades, it was probably just fear.

A Practical approach for Intuitive Financial Decisions

Here's a step-by-step process you can use next time you're facing a financial decision. Write it down. Keep it somewhere accessible. Use it until it becomes second nature.

  1. Gather the facts. Do the logical work first. Get all the data. Crunch the numbers. Talk to people who know more than you.
  2. Step away. Close the laptop. Put down the phone. Go for a walk. Take a shower. Give your conscious mind a break.
  3. Check in with your body. Sit quietly for a minute. Take three deep breaths. Bring the decision to mind. Notice what happens in your body. Tightness? Expansion? Numbness? Something else?
  4. Name the signal. Is this a "hell no" or a "quiet yes"? Or is it something in between? Don't judge it. Just name it.
  5. Ask one question. "If I knew I couldn't fail, what would I do?" This question bypasses fear and gets to the truth. Your answer will tell you a lot.
  6. Decide with both. Use your logic and your intuition together. If they agree, great. If they disagree, don't force it. Wait. Gather more information. Give yourself permission to not decide right now.

There's a book that walks through this exact kind of process in more depth. It's called Playing Big by Tara Mohr. It's written for women, but the principles apply to anyone who's been taught to doubt themselves. See on Amazon (paid link)

What to Do When You Get It Wrong

You will get it wrong sometimes. That's not a sign that your intuition is broken. It's a sign that you're human. Every intuitive person I know has made a bad call. The difference between people who develop their intuition and people who don't is simple: the people who develop it don't let one mistake shut them down.

When you get it wrong, don't beat yourself up. Don't decide that you're "bad at intuition." Instead, ask yourself: What was I actually feeling in that moment? Was it fear? Was it wishful thinking? Was it a signal I misinterpreted? Learn from it. Adjust. And try again.

The worst thing you can do is go back to ignoring your feelings entirely. That's what most people do. They make one mistake, decide their intuition is unreliable, and go back to making decisions from their head alone. And then they wonder why they feel disconnected, anxious, and stuck. Does that land?

Your intuition is like any other skill. It gets better with practice. It gets clearer with use. The more you trust it, the more it shows up for you. But you have to be willing to make mistakes along the way.

Final Thoughts: The Bright Wound

You know what the bright wound is? It's the thing about you that the world told you was a weakness, but is actually your greatest strength. For sensitive people, that wound is often your intuition. You were told you were too emotional. Too sensitive. Too irrational. You were told to stop feeling and start thinking. But the truth is, your ability to feel deeply is exactly what makes you wise.

Money decisions don't have to be cold and clinical. You don't have to shut down your feelings to be "smart" with money. In fact, the smartest financial decisions often come from people who have learned to integrate their logic with their intuition. They use both. They trust both. And they don't apologize for it.

If you're ready to go deeper into this work, I'd recommend The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk. It's not about intuition per se, but it's about how your nervous system stores information that your conscious mind doesn't have access to. Understanding that is key to trusting your gut. See on
